speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get to the United States and how?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went to the University of Heidelberg-- I don't know if I said that before-- for a year, from the DP camps, on my own. And I was primarily in two DP camps. One was Foehrenwald, that was near Dachau. And the second one was Bensheim, and that was near Darmstadt and Heidelberg and Frankfurt. And when I ended up in Bensheim, I waited. And still there was no exit for me to America in the foreseeable future. And so I decided to go to the University of Heidelberg, hoping that I would get a student visa to be able to go to America. I must say that the Germans were wonderful, giving me every break and allowing me to go as a full student, with so little formal education. They helped me. They got me tutors. And I attended the university for an entire year. But meanwhile, my family in the United States worked very hard to get me to join them here. But it still took three years. When I arrived here, it was June 25, 1948. I came on the And it was a very slow boat. And I was ill most of the voyage. I was motion--

speaker: interviewer
text: Nauseous--

speaker: interviewee
text: --seasick.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when I came, I was greeted by my family in New York. I still had five aunts and uncles. The aunts were my father's sisters. And- may I say their names? Absolutely.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. It was Aunt Sarah, Aunt Ida, Aunt Belka, Aunt Ania-- Anna-- and Aunt Bertha. All four-- the four out of the five lived-- lived in the area-- in the New York area. And Aunt Bertha was the only one who lived in Columbus, Ohio. She was the only one who was financially able to support me. And so she came from Columbus, Ohio to meet me at the port and decided without asking me-- but I had no say in it anyway-- and decided to take me with her to Columbus, Ohio. She went through a very difficult time in her life at that time also because she had just recently lost her husband. But anyway, she was very good to me. And she tried to give me all the love and care that I needed desperately. I must pay tribute to my cousin, her only daughter, Rosalie, who probably suffered because of me. There came a stranger, who didn't know a word of English. I may as well have come from Mars. I mean, it was a culture shock for me, as well as for them. And yet, she had to share the love of her mother, my aunt, with a stranger, namely me. It was difficult. I'm not going to gloss over it. It was very difficult in the beginning. And my attitude was sort of-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't really know how to behave and what to do. And so I was very nonplussed, I guess, by the whole thing. They tried very hard. And they didn't know how to accommodate me and how-- how to make me feel comfortable. They wanted to. But I did not appreciate it, because I didn't know how to accept it. I guess that's the only way to say it. The first thing they did is they cut off my hair. And they-- they bought me new clothes that I didn't like, you know. And they threw me into school. And my life started from the very beginning all over again. I wasn't ready to share my experiences. And my family didn't ask for details. Now I realize for two reasons-- not because they weren't interested, but they didn't want to hurt me. And they felt that I wasn't ready probably to share these experiences.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it worked both ways. I didn't-- I was misunderstood. And they didn't know how to react to me. But I don't doubt that they loved me very much. And how long did you stay with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed with my aunt until I married my husband. And that was two and a half years later. I met my husband-- who came to visit some family, he's from New York originally. He came to visit his family and cousins in Columbus, Ohio. And they came to a party, and I was asked to that party. I was going to Ohio State at that time. And I really didn't want to go. But somehow I did come, and we made a connection. I played hard to get in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name-- his full name is Erwin. I don't like his first name, and he knows it. Erwin spelled E-R-W-I-N, which to me connotes Erwin Rommel. And so I've never called him by his name. He's known by the name of Ed. But I very rarely call him by that name. I call him honey, sweetheart. And that's what he's been to me for the last 45 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married on April 15, 1951. And that was a date that was not picked by me. This is a date that commemorates the anniversary of the marriage of my beloved husband's parents and grandparents as well. So it's sort of a tradition. It wasn't picked up by our son. He did not get married on April 15th.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was your son born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our son was born in 1956, September 22nd.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Stephen Frank. And the Hebrew name is Simcha Fruma, after my parents. And he's been a wonderful miracle, if I may say so. I lost five. And I was told I will never carry a child full term. And after 11 months, our miracle baby arrived breach. But he turned out to be a wonderful boy. My married life has been bittersweet in one respect. My husband was called back in the active-- he was in the reserves. And he was called back into active service after we got married. We got married April 15th, and May 12th he was called back into service. And his first assignment was Germany. And that was not a very happy thing for me. So I refused to join him right away. I insisted on becoming an American citizen. And then I joined them in Germany. It was difficult for me to live in Germany. But as an American citizen and as a wife of an officer, it was a little different than living in a DP camp. My husband was a career military man. And his assignments took us to many different places. I say we lived on three continents-- Europe, America, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Asia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Asia. We lived in-- Our son spoke Japanese before he spoke English. But anyway, it's been a wonderful life. My husband had two tours of duty in Vietnam. And that was very difficult for me, to be by myself and to worry about him. He had many close calls. And thank God he-- he-- he came back in one piece. Our son has given us immeasurable joy throughout his life and continues to do so. And now I'm a very proud grandmother of two wonderful little boys. And when we talk with my son, he keeps saying see, you have two little Schiffs running around. That's your legacy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their names-- the older one, who's four years of age, is Perry Tchiya. Now, Perry is named after the maiden name of my daughter, my beloved daughter-in-law, Sharon. And Tchiya is my sister's name. And the younger one is called-- is named Morgan Daniel. And Morgan is after my paternal grandfather's name and also my daughter-in-law's grandfather. So, there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you talk to your son as he was growing up about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I talked to him slowly, I mean, in-- in-- in generalities, little by little. He's been a very compassionate and a very understanding and a very good son. And I'm not just saying that, but it's true. He-- I think we must be very proud, my husband and I, that we instilled in him the right values and what's important in life. He-- from the time he was four years of age, he said he would like to be a surgeon. And he did carry out his goal-- goals. I-- in retrospect, I know I've been too demanding. And I've been-- I've tried perhaps to live my life, my young life, through him. And I was demanding. I always expected him to be perfect. And I wanted him to attend the best schools. I wanted him to be the best of everything. And he did not disappoint us. He went to Princeton undergraduate. And then he did get in on early--

speaker: interviewer
text: Admission.

speaker: interviewee
text: --admission to medical school in Virginia. And then he did his training at Mass General. That's Harvard. And then his first job was on the staff and faculty of the medical school at Yale. And now he's in private practice in Rhode Island and still is also assistant clinical professor at Brown Medical School. So that list-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Pretty--

speaker: interviewee
text: is-- But, I mean, I must admit it, I was probably very hard on him, harder than an ordinary mother. Because I expected so much from him. But he's been a terrific son. He's never been on drugs, never drank, he never smoked. If he did some wrong things, I don't know about them. One thing I do know. The first time he took the car, he promised to call at 10 o'clock in the evening. And he never called. He forgot, and I was sitting at the top of the stairs and bawled him out when he got home. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, looking back at all of the experiences that you had and just having spoken about them in the way that you did, I know you have strong feelings about telling your story. And I'd like, if you could, to share that with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for allowing me to do that. Yes, I do have very strong feelings. Until 19-- when-- when they laid the cornerstone in Washington for the museum, I think it was 1983-- I really didn't know how to fit my life in. I was an army wife. I've never been-- oh not never, but I had very little contact with other survivors. And I didn't know where my niche in life was. I-- I knew-- oh I thought I was a good wife, good army wife, a good mother. But beyond that, I did not know. And then we had a wonderful rabbi. He is since retired, but a good [NON-ENGLISH]. And one day, he dragged me into his confirmation class. He says, now, Charlene-- he knew my background, I didn't advertise it, but he knew my background. And he says, now, Charlene, you must tell your story to my confirmation class. And I was utterly-- I mean, I was scared. I said, I don't know. My English isn't good. I-- I don't have it in my head chronologically. Next time, next year. He says, no. You're going to go do it right now. And he forced me to tell my story-- I mean, just an overview of my story-- to his confirmation class. And he really-- I owe him so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Rabbi Sheldon Elster. And he, I mean, he was a friend as well as our rabbi. We met him-- he used to be the chaplain, the military chaplain. And that's the way we met in the very beginning. And he forced me to tell my story. And he put me on the road of being a spokesperson. Now, I'm sitting here and sharing my experiences with you. And by the way, Ellen, you've made me feel so good and so at ease that it was easy--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to tell my story. I mean it. I couldn't have done it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --if you hadn't been so good. But he made me think about what I needed to do. And I feel that I am a spokesperson. I feel it's my mandate and I-- it's my duty to tell my story. Because I speak for the millions who never had a chance. And I owe it to them, because I survived. And perhaps this is one of the purposes for my survival. I also would like to thank my wonderful husband. Because without his support and understanding-- and I am not easy to live with, I'm sure-- but he has been blind for all our married life, to look away at my shortcomings [SOBBING] and just to emphasize the good parts of my person. And I'm forever grateful. And I could never, ever, repay him for what he's doing for me. And every marriage, they say, is 50-50. Mine is 90-10. I get 90% and give back-- because I'm not able to-- I can only give back 10%. But I give it with my whole heart and my whole being.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I know you're going to introduce us to him in a moment. But first, you have some poems you'd like to share, if you would, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if you could tell us also the occasion upon which you wrote them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, I must go back to my wonderful rabbi, Sheldon Elster from [NON-ENGLISH] in Alexandria, Virginia. He prompted me and encouraged me to tell my story. Because he thought it was very important. And at that time, the world at large became more interested in what happened during the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he encouraged me to do something special. And I said I wasn't learned, and I wasn't well-versed in Hebrew and all. But I said I would like to become a bat mitzvah, a Yom Hashoah bat mitzvah, to honor my parents and my sister and the six million Jews and the others who perished during the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so within three months, he taught me all that I needed to know. I chanted my haftarah. I did all the blessings before and after. And I learned Mussaf. And I did practically the entire service, and all this thanks to Dr. Sheldon Elster, who's also a rabbi. And on the occasion of my Yom Hashoah bat mitzvah in 1985, I got up around 2 o'clock in the morning, and I wrote a poem. And this is the poem. 'I remember blowing bubbles in the air, rainbow colors, all so fair. Nightingales and jasmine scent, all that love and beauty meant. I remember rainbow colors, no, no more. Guards with rifles by the door, Star of David on my coat. I can't swim, I can't float. I remember a haystack in a farmer's field, used by seven as a shield. Then only one of us is left, filled with sorrow and bereft. I remember the bottom of a water well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did someone see me, will they tell? I'm slipping, clinging to the rounded wall. Dear God, don't let me fall. I remember being hungry, snow and frost, cold, alone, and very lost. Why go on with such a life, stalked by terror's cutting knife? I remember my heart by now an empty shell, from all that pain, from all that hell.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's such a long and awful war, my wounds forever an open sore.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES 7 SECONDS] Papa's hug and Mama's kiss, darling sister I'll always miss. Their loving, sweet and gentle faces gaze at me from empty spaces. They're gone forever. All is vanished, and my soul to torment, banished.' Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: May I read? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have one other poem that I wrote in 1992. And that was as an answer to someone who told me, enough already. No more talk about the Holocaust. I teach now in high schools and wherever I'm asked, in churches. And I do feel this is my-- one of my life's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mandates.

speaker: interviewee
text: --mandates. And I must inform the world. I must not allow the world to forget what happened. And this is one of the poems I wrote. 'Wings of My Youth.' 'Wings of my youth, wasted, misspent. Carry me back to that beautiful land, where the world is sane and there is no pain. Where the sun on every one beams and granted are unfulfilled dreams. Wings of my childhood, wings of my youth, crushed and crumpled underfoot. You never had a chance to soar, lost to Hitler's demonic war. The chimneys of Auschwitz stand tall and mute to tell their story, if only they could. The smell of burning flesh and bone turns my aching heart to stone. Six million souls, martyrs are all. Their silence, to me, A thundering call, To bear witness and never forget the unspeakable horror my people has met.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Now you're going to introduce your husband to us, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that is a great pleasure for me to introduce my beloved husband, Ed.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we just have to stop the tape first. Charlene, could you introduce us to the gentleman sitting next to you please?

speaker: interviewee
text: It will give me great pleasure to introduce my husband, Ed Schiff. And I must add that I have-- there are no adjectives in my vocabulary to explain how wonderful he's been to me throughout all these wonderful years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ed, I understand you have something you'd like to share with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, thank you. I would like to say something that hopefully compliments what Charlene has said. And Charlene's story is truly a message for the world. Her experiences speak to the preachers of hate and violence and tells them that no matter how hard they try, there will be those whose spirit and determination will stop them from carrying out their destruction. Her message goes to the young people of the world, inform them and teach them, never forget what can happen if there is apathy, indifference, injustice, and intolerance. Her words go to all the people of the world, asking them not to be afraid to speak up when hate and intolerance raises their ugly heads in our midst. Charlene's survival is an inspiration to all the peoples of goodwill who will know that the human spirit is indomitable, one must never give up, and that ultimately one is not alone. But there's a cost and a price to pay. Her experiences have exacted a terrible penalty on Charlene's health and psyche. The mental anguish, the physical pain, the personal tragedy of loss of family, the uncertainty of not knowing whether every minute, hour, day would be her last, have tested her beyond belief. As her husband, I consider myself to be the luckiest person, excuse me, in the world. I have drawn from her strength, patience, understanding, concern, and love. [SOB]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you both. You have some photos now to share with us. Thank you both, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. From the left to right, on the left side of the picture is my mother, Fruma Lieberman Perlmutter. In the middle is my sister, Tchiya Perlmutter. And on the right hand side is my father, Simcha Perlmutter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately when was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not quite sure. But this is a composite of three separate pictures, and they were probably taken around 1938 or beginning of '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where would they have been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were taken in Poland. And I located them through my aunts here in the United States. This is a composite of three pictures. And they were put together from negatives in Japan, when my husband and I were stationed in Japan in the '50s, in the 1950s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture represents my legacy. From left to right is my beloved older grandson Perry Tchiya. He is named-- Tchiya is after my sister, and Perry is after my beloved daughter-in-law Sharon's family name. The next one is my beloved daughter-in-law, Sharon, Sharon Perry Schiff. And next to her is our beloved son Stephen Frank Schiff, Dr. Stephen Frank Schiff. He is named after my parents. Stephen for Simcha and Frank, Fruma, for my mother. And the last but not least is our beloved grandson, Morgan Daniel, who was named Morgan, Mordechai, after my paternal grandfather and my daughter-in-law's grandfather. And Daniel is for no one in particular, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken a few months ago--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Barrington, Rhode Island, where my family resides, my son and family reside. These are my two beloved grandchildren, our pride and joy. From left to right, left is Morgan Daniel, who is two years old. And on the right is Perry Tyler, who is four years old. And this was taken in 1994 in Barrington, Rhode Island.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you so very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. I'm sorry I stumbled on these things, I don't know.
